questions.question: What usually makes a senior application-backend answer feel stronger than a mid-level one?
options: It clarifies the contract, sizes the workload, and chooses trade-offs deliberately instead of listing components quickly.
It draws the most boxes in the first two minutes.
It avoids making assumptions at all costs.
It always recommends the same queue-plus-cache architecture.
questions.explanation: Structure and judgment matter more than sheer component count.

questions.question: Why is it risky to skip rough sizing in product-backend interviews?
options: Because without traffic and skew estimates, architecture choices become arbitrary and hard to defend.
Because sizing is only needed for storage systems.
Because interviewers only care about exact monthly cloud cost.
Because sizing automatically reveals the final schema.
questions.explanation: Sizing is what anchors the rest of the design.

questions.question: What is the best reason to choose only one or two deep dives in an interview answer?
options: It shows intentional depth on the real risk areas while preserving time for the whole system.
It avoids discussing APIs and data models entirely.
It guarantees the interviewer will not ask follow-ups.
It makes observability unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Depth is valuable when it is aimed at the dominant risk.

questions.question: Which omission most strongly suggests the answer is still incomplete even if the architecture sounds plausible?
options: No failure-mode analysis or observability plan.
No mention of a specific cloud vendor.
No hand-drawn diagram colors.
No estimate of office Wi-Fi speed.
questions.explanation: Operational reasoning is part of the bar for strong answers in this repo.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with picking one crisp assumption set when the prompt is vague?
options: The answer stays coherent, but you may need to acknowledge which other assumptions would change the design.
It guarantees the interviewer cannot challenge the design.
It removes the need for clarification questions.
It ensures there is only one correct architecture.
questions.explanation: Assumptions create clarity, but they should still be surfaced explicitly.

questions.question: Which metric best tests whether the learner is actually thinking about skew in an answer?
options: They can name a concrete concentration signal such as hot keys, celebrity fanout, or priority-queue backlog by class.
They know the default port for WebSockets.
They can list five database brands quickly.
They avoid using the word 'latency'.
questions.explanation: Skew must show up in a measurable way, not just as a buzzword.

questions.question: Why is rollout or migration thinking valuable in product-backend interviews?
options: It shows the design can be introduced safely in a real system instead of only existing as a greenfield sketch.
It proves the candidate memorized deployment tools.
It replaces the need for requirements.
It guarantees zero regressions.
questions.explanation: Real systems rarely appear from scratch with no migration path.

questions.question: What is the strongest closing move after walking through an application-backend architecture?
options: Summarize the main trade-off, name a likely redesign trigger, and explain how you would detect it.
Repeat the component list more slowly.
Claim the design scales indefinitely.
Avoid mentioning any weaknesses so the answer sounds confident.
questions.explanation: A strong close shows judgment, limits, and operational awareness.
